ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'could have been given' expresses a possible event without negation; no negator applies to this gram, so ClNegPol is not instantiated.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'could have been given to the poor' contains no overt temporal adverbial (lexical, phrasal, or clausal) that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The gram presents a hypothetical event ('could have been given') as a possible occurrence in the subject's life history without asserting that a resulting state currently holds; it is counterfactual and does not entail a change of possession that persists at reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is counterfactual/conditional ('could have been given') expressing an unrealized possibility rather than an actual change of state that holds at the reference time; no transition to a resulting state is entailed in reality.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (giving money to the poor) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the modal 'could have' expresses a counterfactual possibility rather than an actual continuous state or activity.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The modal perfect passive 'could have been given' presents a bounded transition (the act of giving to the poor) as a completed whole event, without profiling internal temporal development, and matches the pattern of passive bounded transitions exemplified in the positive cases.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The modal auxiliary 'could' combined with the perfect infinitive 'have been given' construes the situation as hypothetical/conditional rather than asserted as realized fact, instantiating irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'could have been given' is a modal perfect construction where the finite operator is the modal 'could' rather than present-tense 'have', making it tense-shifted per the edge_cases rule.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'could have been given' instantiates VcPass: it contains the passive auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle 'given', the grammatical subject 'the money' is the semantic patient (recipient of the giving), and the agent is omitted.

FullText: » Truly I tell you everywhere the good news is preached through out the whole world , that which this woman has done will be spoken of for a memorial of her . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has done' introduces an overt internal argument ('that which this woman has done'), where the relative pronoun 'that which' functions as the direct object, encoding an affected patient/theme directed from the external argument (the woman) toward the internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has done' licenses an external argument (the woman as agent) and requires an internal object argument (what she did), violating the one-participant internal-argument-only requirement of ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has done' licenses an internal argument (the implied object of the action performed by the woman), violating the requirement that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The act 'has done' refers to the woman's deed (anointing Jesus), which involves a durative process leading to a defined culminating result, classifying it as an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'has done' denotes a punctual transition into a completed action with a definable result state (a memorial deed), construed as a boundary event rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has done' refers to a completed action with a defined result state (the woman's deed), which will be memorialized; this entails a specific endpoint and result, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for bounded events with inherent endpoints.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has done' entails a resulting change of state (a memorial action with lasting consequences) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (a specific action performed by the woman) that involves change and eventive unfolding, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state and dynamic activity.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'that which this woman has done' presents an action that has actually occurred in the actual world (the woman performed a deed), with no indication of counterfactuality; it is a factual statement about a completed action.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has done' expresses a positive eventuality (the woman performed an action); no negator negates this gram—the sentence contains no negation affecting 'has done'.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt temporal adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval; 'will be spoken of' is future-oriented, not a past temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'that which this woman has done will be spoken of for a memorial of her' presents the action as having a resulting state (being spoken of as a memorial) that is relevant to future discourse and the narrative's point, triggering the edge_cases rule for narrative progression.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'that which this woman has done will be spoken of for a memorial of her' reports a past action without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; the focus is on the future commemoration of the deed, not on a present state resulting from the action itself.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'this woman has done' describes a discrete bounded event (a specific action performed by the woman) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for bounded events.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has done' presents a completed action as a bounded whole without internal temporal development; the predicate denotes a transition (the woman's action) construed as a single accomplished event, matching the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'that which this woman has done' asserts a realized action in the actual world as a completed fact, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context overriding this assertion.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has done' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'done' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has done' is active voice (agent subject 'woman' performs the action); it lacks passive morphology ('have been' + past participle) required for VcPass.

FullText: » The Son of man goes even as it is written of him . Woe to that man through whom the Son of man is betrayed ! It would have been better if he had not been born . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'would have been better' functions as a copula construction linking the subject to a predicative complement (better), not introducing an internal argument, thus failing the intuition criterion.
ASUncc_explanation: The copular verb 'be' with the predicate adjective 'better' encodes a one-participant state with the subject as internal argument, licenses no external argument, and matches the positive example pattern of copular 'be' clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'be' in 'would have been better' denotes a state of being/existence and does not encode a one-participant event with an external argument; it is a copular construction with a predicative adjective complement, not an unergative verb.
AktAcc_explanation: The target gram 'would have been better' expresses a stative counterfactual condition rather than a dynamic, durative event culminating in a defined terminal point, so it does not instantiate AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'would have been better' expresses a stative condition or comparative evaluation rather than a dynamic change-of-state event with a punctual threshold transition; it describes a hypothetical state, not a boundary crossing into a new result state.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a stative predicate expressing a result state or condition, lacking dynamic unfolding and sustained engagement, thus failing the intuition criterion for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a stative predicate expressing a resulting state or condition, not a dynamic punctual event, and therefore does not instantiate the semelfactive feature.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would have been better' denotes a durative stative condition (a comparative attribute/state) with no dynamic change, internal unfolding, or eventive progression, matching the intuition for AktState despite its counterfactual conditional form.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'would have been better' is the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional expressing an unfulfilled, contrary-to-fact situation: the person was born (actual fact), but the utterance asserts it would have been better if he had not been born (contrary to fact).
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have been better' expresses a positive comparative state; negation applies to the conditional clause 'if he had not been born', not to the target gram itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains a conditional marker ('if') that does not establish a specific temporal anchor, and there is no overt temporal adverbial (lexical, phrasal, or clausal) explicitly anchoring the event to a past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The construction 'would have been better' presents a counterfactual conditional state (a hypothetical better condition), not an experiential event in the subject's life history; it introduces a resulting state of affairs rather than recording whether an event occurred.
DtRes_explanation: The clause contains a stative copula construction ('would have been better') that describes a hypothetical condition rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state that holds at the reference time; per edge_cases, copular constructions describe states, not changes of state.
DtUniv_explanation: The target expresses a counterfactual conditional about a state transition (not being born), focusing on the resulting state rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and does not denote a continuous activity or habitual series from a past point to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'better' denotes a stative quality without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not introduce a transition into or out of that state, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for stative situations.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a conditional/counterfactual construction expressing a hypothetical, non-actual situation contrary to fact, instantiating irrealis mood.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' is a modal perfect construction with 'would' (modal finite operator) + 'have' + past participle, which instantiates TLTS because the finite operator is modal rather than present-tense 'have'.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the adjective 'better' (a non-verbal predicate), making this a copular construction rather than a passive voice construction; passive voice requires 'been' + past participle functioning as a verbal passive, not an adjectival predicate.

FullText: » The Son of man goes even as it is written of him . Woe to that man through whom the Son of man is betrayed ! It would have been better if he had not been born . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'be born' functions as a copula-like construction introducing a predicative state rather than a transitive event with an overt internal argument; the passive construction 'had not been born' lacks an overt affected patient in the syntactic structure.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had not been born'), and the lexical verb 'bear/born' licenses an external argument (an implicit agent who would do the bearing), triggering the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses with external argument licensing.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'born' denotes a change-of-state/coming-into-being event where the sole argument (he) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) rather than an external argument, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Be born' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the moment of birth) without a durative process phase inherent to the event itself, classifying it as an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had not been born' denotes a punctual transition into the state of existence, representing a definable threshold moment of coming into being, which qualifies as an achievement despite any preceding biological process.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'had not been born' denotes a single punctual event marking the onset of existence (or lack thereof), which is a bounded perceptual/cognitive event with a defined result state, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had not been born' entails a transition into a resulting state (existence vs. non-existence) and unfolds over a duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' is marked with progressive aspect ('had been') and denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of existence, both of which trigger set_to_0_if conditions.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had not been born' appears in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('if he had not been born') expressing an unfulfilled, contrary-to-fact condition about a person who was actually born, parallel to the apodosis 'It would have been better.'
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had not been born' directly instantiates negation of the eventuality (birth), with 'not' negating the passive perfect construction expressing the event itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'if he had not been born' contains a conditional marker ('if') that does not establish a specific temporal anchor, and there is no overt temporal adverbial present.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'be born' entails a change of state (from non-existence to existence) that results in a permanent condition holding at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The stative copula construction 'had not been born' describes a state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; it is a negated existential condition in a counterfactual conditional, not a change of state with persisting results.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'he had not been born' describes a transition into a new state (non-existence/non-birth) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the focus is on that transition rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'he had not been born' is negated with 'not', indicating the transition has not occurred at the reference time, and the situation remains open rather than presenting a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'had not been born' is embedded within a counterfactual conditional structure ('It would have been better if he had not been born'), which projects a hypothetical, non-actual situation rather than asserting a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' is a past perfect construction (had + past participle), which encodes tense specification other than simple present perfect, triggering TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had not been born' instantiates VcPass: it contains the passive auxiliary sequence 'had been' + past participle 'born', the grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic patient (undergoer of birth), and the agent is omitted, satisfying all conditions for passive voice morphology.

FullText: He took a cup , and when he had given praise , he gave it to them and they all had a drink from it .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had given' introduces an overt internal argument 'praise' (direct object), encoding a two-participant transitive event directed from the external argument (He) toward the internal argument (praise).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'give' in 'had given praise' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he') and introduces an overt direct object ('praise'), violating both set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'give' licenses an internal argument ('praise' as direct object), violating the requirement that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Give praise' (i.e., offer thanks/blessing) is construed as a punctual act of utterance without a durative process phase leading to a defined terminal result state.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had given praise' is a punctual transition into a new result state (praise has been offered/completed) that serves as a temporal boundary point before the subsequent action 'he gave it to them', meeting the achievement criterion.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'had given praise' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (the completion of giving praise) and constitutes a single punctual event with a defined result state, not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had given praise' entails a transition into a resulting state (praise has been given/completed), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had given praise' denotes a bounded dynamic event (giving) involving change-of-state and internal progression, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'when he had given praise' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative sequence (he gave praise before giving the cup), not to indicate a counterfactual situation; the event actually occurred in the narrative.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had given praise' is not negated; the sentence presents the event as occurring in reality without any negator applying to it.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'when he had given praise' contains the clausal temporal adverbial 'when', which explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval relative to the main clause action.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had given praise' entails a completed action that sets the narrative scene for the subsequent main action ('he gave it to them'), making it part of narrative progression rather than a life-history experiential entry.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'when he had given praise' reports a past interaction without entailing a persisting resulting state at the reference time; praise given in the past does not remain 'in effect' in the way that broken objects or received donations do.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'when he had given praise' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of giving praise) that serves as a temporal marker for another event, not a continuous situation holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The pluperfect 'had given praise' presents the act of giving praise as a completed, bounded transition event that occurred prior to the subsequent action of giving the cup, construing it as a discrete whole rather than profiling internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'when he had given praise' is embedded in a temporal sequence of realized events (he took a cup, gave praise, gave it to them, they drank), all asserted as actual facts in the narrative, with no modal, conditional, or hypothetical context.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had given praise' is a past perfect construction (had + past participle), which encodes past tense rather than the simple present perfect, thus instantiating TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'had given praise' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'he' as the semantic agent (doer) and 'praise' as the object; it lacks the passive morphology of 'been' + past participle required for VcPass.

FullText: He came a third time and said to them : » Sleep and take your rest . It is enough the hour has come . Look , the Son of man is betrayed into the hands of sinners !
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has come' is an intransitive verb that does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object), and the event is not construed as directed toward an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has come' encodes a change of location/state with no external argument licensed; the sole argument (the hour) functions as an internal argument (theme), matching the intuition for ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' in 'the hour has come' denotes a change-of-state or appearance event where the sole argument (the hour) functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing the state change, not as an external argument/agent.
AktAcc_explanation: 'The hour has come' denotes a punctual boundary transition — the arrival of a designated moment — without a durative process phase leading to it, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'the hour has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the arrival of a specific moment in time), representing a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual, bounded perceptual event marking the onset of a specific moment in time ('the hour has come'), which lacks dynamic durative unfolding and constitutes a single completed occurrence rather than a sustained activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'the hour has come' entails a transition into a new resulting state (the hour arriving/beginning), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The phrase 'has come' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness or completion of a change-of-state (the arrival of 'the hour'), which involves dynamic change and eventive progression incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the hour has come' presents a situation as actually occurring in the narrative world (the hour has indeed arrived), not as contrary to fact, so it does not instantiate ClCntfcl.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has come' expresses a realized event (the hour has arrived) with no negator present in the sentence to negate this eventuality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'the hour has come' contains no overt temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the adverbial requirement is not met.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has come' entails a change of state (the hour transitioning from not-arrived to arrived), and that resulting state holds at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has come' is a motion verb indicating arrival, but the context shows the hour has arrived and passed (the betrayal is now happening), so the mover/event has moved beyond the resulting state rather than persisting in it, classifying as non-resultative.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come' describes a punctual transition into a new state (the arrival/occurrence of 'the hour'), not a continuous situation holding over time, matching the negative example 'I have broken the vase.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has come' denotes a transition event (arrival of an hour) construed as a completed, bounded whole without internal temporal development, matching the positive example 'He has died.'
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'the hour has come' asserts a realized fact in the actual world within the narrative context, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish operators present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has come' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'come' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has come' is active voice (subject 'hour' is the semantic agent/experiencer of the intransitive verb 'come'), lacking the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: We have heard his words against God . What is your opinion ? They all said he should be put to death .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have heard' introduces an overt internal argument ('his words against God'), satisfying the requirement for a two-participant transitive event with an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an external argument (the subject 'we' as agent/experiencer) and introduces an overt direct object ('his words'), violating both set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an internal argument ('his words'), violating the requirement that no internal argument be licensed for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual/cognitive transition into awareness of the words, construed as a single boundary event without a durative process phase leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness of his words, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news' and satisfying the intuition of a boundary-point transition rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness of 'his words against God', which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have heard' entails a resulting state (knowledge/awareness of having perceived the words) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of awareness (hearing his words), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if rule for perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'We have heard his words against God' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (they genuinely heard the words), with no indication of counterfactuality or contrary-to-fact meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have heard' is not negated; the sentence affirms that 'we have heard his words' without any negator applying to this eventuality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'We have heard his words against God' contains no overt temporal adverbial (lexical, phrasal, or clausal) that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'have heard' presents the event of hearing his words as an entry in the subject's life history without asserting that a resulting state (such as continued auditory perception) holds at the reference time; it answers whether the event occurred, matching the positive example 'We have seen the Eiffel Tower.'
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have heard' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it merely reports a past interaction of perceiving words, similar to the negative example 'They have visited Paris.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'have heard his words' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of hearing/perceiving his words) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, matching the negative example 'I have broken the vase.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have heard' presents the auditory event as a bounded, completed whole without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'We have heard his words against God' asserts a realized, completed action in the actual world with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applied.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'heard' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have heard' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'We' as the semantic agent (doer) and 'his words' as the grammatical object/semantic patient; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle, which is absent here.

FullText: Pilate said : » Why , what evil has he done ? « But they cried out exceedingly , » Impale him ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has done' introduces an overt internal argument 'evil' (direct object), encoding a two-participant transitive event directed from the external argument (he) toward the affected internal argument (evil).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he' as agent) and takes a direct object ('evil'), violating both the intuition and set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has done' licenses an internal argument (the direct object 'evil'), violating the requirement that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'has done (evil)' implies a durative process of committing wrongdoing leading to a defined result state (evil accomplished), fitting the accomplishment profile, though the specific act is unspecified and could in some contexts be punctual.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'has done (evil)' unfolds over a measurable duration as a process of committing wrongful acts rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, and it can naturally occur as the complement of 'finish' (e.g., 'He finished doing evil'), both of which trigger set_to_0_if conditions.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has done evil' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness of wrongdoing with a defined result state (evil committed), not a dynamic durative activity without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has done (evil)' entails a resulting change of state or condition (committing wrongdoing with consequences), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting state change.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a bounded dynamic event (a completed action with internal development and a clear endpoint), which violates the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state and eventive verbs.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what evil has he done' is an embedded interrogative introduced by 'what' (functioning as an indirect question within Pilate's direct speech), asking about actual events that may have occurred, not presenting a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negator 'what' does not negate the target gram 'has done'; rather, the question presupposes that evil has been done and asks which evil, making the eventuality realized rather than absent.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'what evil has he done' contains no overt temporal adverbial (lexical, phrasal, or clausal) that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'what evil has he done' queries whether an event of wrongdoing has occurred in the subject's history without asserting a resulting state; it focuses on the occurrence itself rather than a persistent consequence.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'what evil has he done' is a question that reports a past action without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it inquires about completed wrongdoing rather than encoding a transition to a state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what evil has he done' describes a discrete bounded event (a specific wrongdoing) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, matching the set_to_0_if condition for 'one or more discrete bounded events.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has done' presents a completed transition (the action of doing evil) as a bounded whole without profiling its internal temporal development, instantiating perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The interrogative form 'What evil has he done?' seeks confirmation of an actual fact within the narrative world (Pilate questioning what wrongdoing has actually occurred), not a hypothetical or projected situation, thus it asserts a realized state and does not instantiate irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has done' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'done', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has done' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'he' as the semantic agent (doer) and the object 'evil' as the semantic patient; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle, which is absent here.

FullText: At the ninth hour Jesus cried with a loud voice , saying : » Eloi , Eloi , lama sabachthani ? « Interpreted it means , » My God , my God , why have you forsaken me ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' introduces an overt internal argument ('me'), encoding a two-participant event where the external argument (God) is construed as directing an action toward and affecting the internal argument (the speaker).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you') and takes a direct object ('me'), violating both the intuition requirement and the set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' licenses an internal argument ('me'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Forsake' denotes a punctual boundary transition (abandonment) without a durative process phase leading to a defined terminal result state, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The perfect form 'have forsaken' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (abandonment/desertion) that crosses a definable threshold, meeting the achievement criterion despite any preceding causation.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (the state of being abandoned/deserted), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for situations with inherent endpoints or result states.
AktSemel_explanation: The perfect aspect 'have forsaken' entails a resulting state of abandonment/separation that persists from past to present, which violates the 'no resulting change of state' requirement for semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a change-of-state (abandonment/separation) involving dynamic progression from a prior state of non-abandonment to abandonment, which violates the stative requirement of no dynamic change or internal eventive unfolding.

DtRes_explanation: The clause 'that had been cut out of a rock' uses a stative copular construction (past participle as adjective) describing the tomb's state rather than encoding a transition; it reports a pre-existing condition at the reference time, not a change of state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a completed action (the cutting of the tomb) resulting in a particular state, focusing on the transition/result rather than a continuous situation holding over time.

FullText: They went to the tomb when the sun had risen . It was very early .

FullText: He said to them : » Do not be amazed . Jesus the Nazarene , who has been impaled , is not here . He has risen . Behold , the place where they laid him !

VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been impaled' exhibits overt passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle 'impaled'), the grammatical subject 'Jesus the Nazarene' is the semantic patient (undergoer of impalement), and the agent is omitted, satisfying all conditions for VcPass.

FullText: She told those who had been with him , for they mourned and wept .

FullText: Afterward he was revealed to the eleven as they sat at the table . He rebuked them for their unbelief and hardness of heart , because they did not believe those who had seen him after he had risen .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had seen' introduces an overt internal argument 'him' (direct object), instantiating a two-participant transitive event directed from the external argument toward an affected internal argument.

FullText: Afterward he was revealed to the eleven as they sat at the table . He rebuked them for their unbelief and hardness of heart , because they did not believe those who had seen him after he had risen .

FullText: Many have attempted to write an orderly narrative about the things that have taken place .

TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have taken place' is embedded in a relative clause asserting realized events in the actual world ('the things that have taken place'), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applying.

FullText: The angel answered : » I am Gabriel , whose place is before God . I have been sent to give you this good news .

FullText: When they saw him they were astonished . His mother said to him : » Son , why did you treat us this way ? Your father and I have searched for you and we have been worried . «

DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have searched' expresses a continuous activity (searching) that has held from a past starting point up to the reference time, without discrete bounded events or numerical limits, fitting the intuition for DtUniv.

FullText: When they saw him they were astonished . His mother said to him : » Son , why did you treat us this way ? Your father and I have searched for you and we have been worried . «

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have been worried' entails a state of emotional distress that persists at the reference time (the mother is still worried as she speaks), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for resulting states that hold at reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'have been worried' is a stative copula (present perfect of 'be' + adjective) that describes a continuing emotional state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; per edge_cases, copular constructions do not encode change of state even if worry resulted from a prior event.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have been worried' expresses a state of worry that has held continuously from a past starting point (when the son went missing) up to the reference time (the present moment of speaking), fitting the intuition of a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have been worried' is a present perfect progressive form profiling the internal temporal development of a stative situation (worry) rather than presenting it as a bounded whole, and the clause lacks a construed boundary or transition, matching the set_to_0_if condition for stative situations without boundaries.

FullText: John reproved Herod the tetrarch for all the evil things he had done for he took his brother Herodias’ wife .

DtRes_explanation: The target 'had done (evil things)' encodes a completed transition to a state of having committed wrongdoing that persists at the reference time (the moment of reproval), making the resulting state relevant to why John reproved Herod.

FullText: Then the devil said : » I will give all this authority and glory to you , for it has been turned over to me . I may give it to whomever I will .

FullText: When the devil had completed every temptation he departed from him for a season .

FullText: » The Spirit of Jehovah is upon me . He anointed me to preach good news to the poor . He has sent me to proclaim release to the captives , and recovering of sight to the blind . To set at liberty those who are bruised .

FullText: » What have we to do with you Jesus , you Nazarene ? Have you come to destroy us ? I know who you are . You are the Holy One of God ! «

FullText: Jesus rebuked him , saying : » Be quiet and come out of him . « When the demon had thrown him down in front of them , he came out of him without harming him .

FullText: When the voice came Jesus was found alone . They kept quiet and told no man what they had seen .

DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what they had seen' describes a discrete bounded event (the witnessing of a specific occurrence) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for bounded events.

FullText: » Woe to you Chorazin ! Woe to you Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon , which were done in you , they would have repented long ago , sitting in sackcloth and ashes .

ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument (the implicit agent performing the works), and 'mighty works' functions as the theme/patient (internal argument) rather than as an external argument, violating the core requirement for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The 'mighty works' (miracles/deeds) denote a set of durative, effortful actions that unfold over time and culminate in defined results, fitting the accomplishment profile of a dynamic, durative process leading to a terminal result state.
AktAch_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a punctual transition into a completed state of miraculous works, representing the boundary point where the works came into existence, satisfying the achievement criterion despite the preparatory activity involved in performing miracles.

DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains a conditional marker ('If') that does not establish a specific temporal anchor; the adverbial 'long ago' modifies the consequent clause ('would have repented long ago'), not the target clause itself.
DtExp_explanation: The clause is counterfactual ('If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon...they would have repented'), and the resulting state of the works being done in those cities is directly relevant to the conditional consequence, making this non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is counterfactual (introduced by 'If') and negates the actual occurrence of the event; no transition to a resulting state is entailed in reality.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (miracles performed in specific locations) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the counterfactual conditional structure focuses on the occurrence of these events as distinct occurrences, not their duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive past perfect 'had been done' presents the transition of mighty works being performed as a bounded, completed event without profiling internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had been done' appears in a counterfactual conditional protasis ('If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon...'), which projects a hypothetical, non-actual situation contrary to fact, triggering irrealis.

VcPass_explanation: The construction 'had been done' exhibits overt passive morphology (auxiliary 'had' + 'been' + past participle 'done'), the grammatical subject 'mighty works' is the semantic patient (undergoer of the action), and the agent is omitted, satisfying all conditions for VcPass.

FullText: » Woe to you Chorazin ! Woe to you Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon , which were done in you , they would have repented long ago , sitting in sackcloth and ashes .

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'repented' entails a change of state (from unrepentant to repentant), and in this counterfactual conditional, the resulting state of repentance would hold at the reference time if the condition were met, triggering the set_to_0_if rule.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is counterfactual (conditional with 'if' and past perfect antecedent) and does not assert that a resulting state actually holds at the reference time; it describes a hypothetical transition that did not occur.

FullText: » Behold , I have given you authority to tread upon serpents and scorpions , and over all the power of the enemy : and nothing shall in any way hurt you .

FullText: » If I use the finger power Spirit of God to cast out demons , then the kingdom of God has come upon you ?

FullText: » Woe to you Pharisees ! You tithe mint and rue and every herb , and pass over justice and the love of God . You should have done these but you leave them undone .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'done' introduces an overt internal argument 'these' (direct object), encoding a two-participant event directed from the external argument (You) toward an affected internal argument (these), satisfying the ASTrns feature.

FullText: » You bear witness that I said I am not the Christ . I have been sent before him .

FullText: » He testifies about what he has seen and heard , but no man accepts his testimony .

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has seen' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (what he has actually witnessed), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation, so it does not instantiate irrealis.

FullText: Jesus answered : » If you know the gift of God and who asked you for a drink , you would have asked him to give you living water . «

FullText: Jesus saw him lying there . He knew that he had been sick for a long time . So he asked him : » Do you want to get well ? «
