FullText: They looked for a way to capture him . But no man laid his hand on him . This is because his hour had not yet arrived .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'arrive' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'his hour' is the subject, not an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'arrive' encodes a one-participant change-of-location event where the sole argument ('hour') functions as an internal argument (theme), with no external argument licensed or overtly realized.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument ('hour') functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing the change, not as an external argument/agent, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Arrived' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the moment of reaching a defined point in time), not a durative process leading to a culmination, matching the set_to_0_if condition for punctual change-of-state verbs.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'arrive' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (the crossing of a temporal threshold when 'his hour' comes), and the negation with 'not yet' emphasizes the boundary point that has not been reached, fitting the achievement definition.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'had not yet arrived' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of a defined moment (the arrival of 'his hour'), which is a single completed occurrence with an inherent endpoint, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for bounded perceptual events and defined result states.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'arrive' entails a transition into a new resulting state (the hour coming/being present), which violates the set_to_0_if condition that excludes events with resulting state changes.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had not yet arrived' denotes a change-of-state event (arrival as a bounded temporal boundary) involving dynamic progression toward an endpoint, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual situation in the actual world (his hour had not yet arrived as the reason why no man laid hands on him), using past perfect for temporal anteriority within narrative without any counterfactual marking.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had not yet arrived' directly negates the eventuality of arrival through the negator 'not', presenting the event as not occurring/not realized.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target contains the overt temporal adverbial 'yet' which explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval, indicating that the arrival had not occurred up to that point.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'arrive' entails a change of state/transition into a new condition (the hour coming/occurring), and the resulting state (the hour not having arrived yet) is the current state of affairs at the reference time, which explains why no one could capture him.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is negated ('had not yet arrived'), so no transition to a resulting state is entailed; the set_to_0_if condition applies.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a state that has not yet occurred (a transition into a future event) rather than a situation holding continuously from a past point to the reference time; the focus is on the non-arrival (absence of transition) rather than on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause contains 'not yet', which explicitly signals that the transition has not occurred at the reference time and the situation remains open, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'his hour had not yet arrived' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world as the reason ('This is because...') for the preceding events, and negation alone does not trigger irrealis per the edge_cases rule.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had not yet arrived' is a past perfect construction (past-tense 'had' + past participle 'arrived'), which encodes a tense specification other than the simple present perfect, triggering TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had not yet arrived' is an active intransitive verb construction where the subject 'hour' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not the patient; there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle sequence).

FullText: Many in the crowd believed in him . They asked : » When the Christ comes will he do more signs than those this man has done ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has done' introduces an overt internal argument ('more signs than those this man has done'), satisfying the two-participant transitive event structure with an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'do' licenses an external argument (agent 'this man') and takes an overt direct object ('signs'), violating both the external argument and direct object conditions in set_to_0_if.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument ('signs' or 'more signs'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant external-argument-only predicate.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has done' here refers to performing signs (miracles), which implies a durative process leading to defined, completed results (the signs themselves as endpoints), fitting the accomplishment profile.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has done' refers to a series of completed miraculous signs/actions that unfolded over time as a process, not a single punctual transition into a new state, and it can be naturally framed as completion of durative activity ('finish doing signs').
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'this man has done' refers to a completed set of signs (miracles) with an inherent endpoint and defined result state, matching the 'set_to_0_if' condition for situations with specific, inherent endpoints.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has done' refers to a cumulative set of signs (plural) performed over time, construing the event as durative and iterative rather than a single punctual occurrence, thus not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'has done' denotes a bounded dynamic event (performing signs/miracles) involving internal eventive unfolding and change, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'this man has done' is an embedded interrogative introduced by 'if/whether' (indirect question about what signs the Christ will do compared to what this man has already done), presenting the situation as actually occurred, not contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has done' is not negated; the sentence presents the man's past actions as realized, not absent, and any negation in the broader sentence ('will he do more signs than those this man has done') does not negate the target gram itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'this man has done' contains no overt temporal adverbial (lexical, phrasal, or clausal) that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'this man has done' presents the performance of signs as an entry in the subject's life history without asserting that a resulting state currently holds; it answers whether the event has occurred, not whether a state persists.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'this man has done' appears in a comparative context ('more signs than those this man has done') reporting past miraculous actions without entailing a persisting resulting state at the reference time, similar to the negative example 'They have visited Paris.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (specific signs/miracles performed) rather than a continuous situation, and the comparison 'more signs than those this man has done' focuses on a count of distinct occurrences.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has done' presents the action of performing signs as a bounded, completed whole event without internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'this man has done' asserts a realized fact about signs already performed in the actual world within the narrative, despite appearing in a question that projects a future comparison.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has done' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'done' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'this man' corresponds to the semantic agent (doer) of 'has done', and there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); this is active voice.

FullText: » No man has ever spoken the way he speaks , « they replied .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has spoken' appears without an overt internal argument (no direct or indirect object is syntactically realized), matching the activity reading condition in set_to_0_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'spoken' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'man' who performs the speaking action), violating the first set_to_0_if condition.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'spoken' licenses an internal argument (the implied object 'the way he speaks' or understood direct object), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb licenses or realizes an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: Although speaking can be durative, 'has spoken' here lacks an inherent endpoint or result state and can be modified by a 'for X time' adverbial without implying completion of a defined goal, making it an atelic activity rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'has spoken' denotes a punctual transition into a state of having communicated (a completed speech act), representing a boundary point rather than an unfolding process, consistent with achievement semantics.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has spoken' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness of speech ('the way he speaks'), which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has spoken' entails a resulting state (the fact of having communicated/uttered words) and unfolds over measurable duration rather than as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has spoken' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the completion of a speech act, which involves dynamic change and eventive progression rather than a static condition that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual statement about what has actually occurred in the world (no man has ever spoken this way), without any indication of counterfactuality or contrary-to-fact meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'No man' negates the subject, not the target gram 'has spoken'; the eventuality of speaking is presented as realized (it occurred in the past), just with no man doing it.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt temporal adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval; 'ever' is a quantifier, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'has spoken' presents the event as part of human life history (whether anyone has ever spoken in a certain way) without asserting a resulting state; the frequency adverbial 'ever' and the comparative context ('the way he speaks') focus on occurrence rather than a persistent result state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has spoken' reports a past interaction without entailing a persisting resulting state at the reference time; it is an experiential perfect indicating that no one has ever had the experience of speaking in this manner, not that a change of state currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (no man has ever spoken in this manner) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the exceptional nature of the speech act itself, not on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has spoken' presents the speech act as a bounded, completed transition event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'No man has ever spoken the way he speaks' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (a general truth about past experience), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has spoken' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'spoken' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has spoken' is active voice (subject 'No man' is the semantic agent/doer), lacking the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » Does our law convict a man without first hearing from him to find out what he has done ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has done' introduces an overt internal argument ('what'), which is a relative pronoun functioning as the direct object of the verb, satisfying the requirement for an overt internal argument encoding an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an external argument (the man as agent performing an action), violating the core requirement that ASUncc verbs encode one-participant events where the sole argument is an internal argument without an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument (the implied object 'what'), as evidenced by the interrogative pronoun 'what' functioning as the direct object of 'has done', which violates the first set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Has done' is a semantically bleached pro-verb whose aspectual class depends entirely on its unspecified complement; in this context it refers to whatever unspecified actions the man may have performed, which could range from punctual to accomplishment-type events, but the most natural reading of 'what he has done' implies a completed action with a result, tentatively supporting an accomplishment reading, though the vagueness of the pro-verb makes confident classification impossible.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has done' is too general and semantically bleached to denote a specific punctual state-change transition; it functions as a light verb placeholder for unspecified actions that may be durative or punctual, failing to meet the achievement criterion of a definable threshold crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'what he has done' refers to a completed action or set of actions with an inherent endpoint or result state (the specific deed(s) committed), not a dynamic durative situation unfolding without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The perfect aspect 'has done' refers to a completed action with relevance to the present state, not a single punctual occurrence; it encompasses the entire duration and consequences of whatever action was performed, thus failing the instantaneous/punctual requirement for semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a bounded dynamic event with internal eventive unfolding and an inherent endpoint, which violates the stative requirement and matches the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state and dynamic activity.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what he has done' is an embedded interrogative introduced by 'what' (functioning as an indirect question within 'to find out'), presenting uncertain factual information rather than a counterfactual situation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has done' is embedded in a relative clause and is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('without') applies to the matrix clause structure, not to the eventuality of 'has done'.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'what he has done' contains no overt temporal adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval; the adverbial 'first' is a logical/procedural connector, not a temporal one.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'what he has done' presents the action as an entry in the subject's life history without asserting a resulting state; it answers whether an action occurred, not what state currently holds.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'what he has done' reports a past action within a relative clause that functions as the object of 'find out'; it does not entail a resulting state that persists at the reference time, but rather describes a completed past event whose consequences are unknown.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what he has done' refers to discrete past actions or events that the law seeks to discover, not a continuous situation holding over time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition about bounded events.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has done' presents a completed action as a bounded whole without profiling its internal development, matching the perfective aspect definition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'what he has done' is embedded within an interrogative seeking confirmation of an actual fact (whether the law convicts without hearing), asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world rather than projecting a hypothetical or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has done' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'done' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has done' is active voice (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'done' with agent as grammatical subject 'he'), not passive voice, which requires the morphological sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: He spoke these words at the treasury as he taught in the temple . No one tried to arrest him because his hour had not yet arrived .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'arrived' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it describes a one-participant event of coming/reaching a location, not a two-participant transitive event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'arrive' encodes a one-participant change-of-location event where the sole argument ('his hour') functions as an internal argument (theme), with no external argument licensed or introduced.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument ('hour') functions as an internal argument (theme) rather than an external argument, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Arrived' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the moment an appointed time comes), not a durative process leading to a defined endpoint, matching the negative example 'The guests have arrived.'
AktAch_explanation: The event 'arrived' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (the crossing of a temporal threshold when 'his hour' comes), matching the intuition of a boundary-point transition regardless of any preceding durative process.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'arrived' denotes a punctual, bounded perceptual event marking the onset of a specific moment (the arrival of 'his hour'), which lacks dynamic durative unfolding and constitutes a single completed occurrence rather than a sustained activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'arrive' entails a transition into a new resulting state (moving from not-arrived to arrived), which violates the set_to_0_if condition for semelfactive aspect.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has not yet arrived' denotes a change-of-state event (arrival as a bounded temporal boundary) involving dynamic progression toward a completion point, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'his hour had not yet arrived' presents a factual situation within the narrative (explaining why no one arrested him), using past perfect for temporal anteriority without any counterfactual marking or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had not yet arrived' directly negates the eventuality of arrival through the negator 'not', presenting the event as not occurring/not realized.
DtDTAM_explanation: The adverbial 'yet' is vague and does not establish a specific past time interval; it only indicates incompleteness relative to an unspecified reference point, failing the requirement for explicit temporal anchoring.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'arrive' entails a change of location/state, and the resulting state (the hour being present/at hand) is relevant to the discourse context explaining why no one arrested him.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is negated ('had not yet arrived'), so no transition to a resulting state is entailed; the set_to_0_if condition applies.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'had not yet arrived' describes a transition into a new state (the arrival event) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the non-occurrence of that punctual event, not on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'his hour had not yet arrived' is negated with 'not yet', indicating the transition has not occurred at the reference time and the situation remains open, which triggers set_to_0_if condition 4.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'his hour had not yet arrived' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world at the narrative moment (a past fact), and negation alone does not trigger irrealis per the edge_cases rule.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had not yet arrived' is a past perfect construction (had + past participle), which encodes past tense marking rather than the canonical present perfect form, thus instantiating TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has not yet arrived' is an active intransitive construction where the grammatical subject 'his hour' is the semantic agent/experiencer; it lacks the passive morphology (have + been + past participle) required for VcPass.

FullText: » Who are you , « they asked . Jesus replied : » What have I been telling you from the beginning ?
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'have been telling' introduces an overt internal argument ('you'), satisfying the two-participant event requirement with an affected patient syntactically realized.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'telling' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I') and introduces an overt direct object ('you'), violating both set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'telling' licenses an internal argument ('you' as indirect object), violating the requirement for zero internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have been telling' describes a dynamic, durative communicative activity ('from the beginning') but lacks an inherent terminal endpoint or result state, making it an activity rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have been telling' unfolds over a measurable duration (from the beginning until now) rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, and it can naturally occur as the complement of 'finish' (e.g., 'finish telling'), both of which trigger set_to_0_if conditions.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have been telling' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of sustained communication unfolding over time ('from the beginning') without an inherent endpoint, fitting the activity (AktAct) profile.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have been telling' unfolds over a measurable duration (from the beginning until now) rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, and the context construes it as a repeated or iterative series of tellings, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have been telling' is morphologically marked as progressive aspect (present perfect progressive), which entails dynamic unfolding and internal eventive progression incompatible with stative readings, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a situation that has actually occurred (Jesus has been telling them things from the beginning), without any indication that it is contrary to fact; it is a factual statement about past events.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have been telling' is not negated; it appears in a direct question without any negator applying to it.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'What have I been telling you from the beginning?' contains no overt temporal adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval; 'from the beginning' is a prepositional phrase indicating duration/scope rather than a discrete temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have been telling' entails an ongoing communicative state (Jesus has been in the process of telling them things from the beginning until now), which represents a continuing situation rather than a discrete life-history entry, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for duration/continuing situations.
DtRes_explanation: The communication verb 'have been telling' reports a past interaction without entailing that the information is currently binding or in effect; it merely describes what was communicated from the beginning, not a persisting resulting state.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have been telling you from the beginning' expresses a continuous activity (telling) that has held from a past starting point ('from the beginning') up to the reference time, matching the intuition for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have been telling' has the form 'have been' + -ing, which profiles the internal temporal development of the situation rather than presenting it as a bounded whole, triggering the first set_to_0_if condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have been telling' is embedded in a direct question ('What have I been telling you from the beginning?') that seeks confirmation of an actual, realized fact within the narrative world, not a hypothetical or projected situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have been telling' is a present perfect progressive form, which includes progressive marking ('been telling') in addition to the present perfect auxiliary 'have', thus instantiating TLTS as it encodes a tense specification beyond the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have been telling' has active voice morphology where the grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent (doer) performing the action of telling, not the patient undergoing the action; passive voice requires the subject to be the semantic patient with the agent in a by-phrase or omitted.

FullText: Jesus said : » When you have lifted up impaled the Son of man you will know that I am that person . I do nothing by myself ! I speak what the Father taught me !
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have lifted up' introduces an overt internal argument ('the Son of man'), satisfying the requirement for a two-participant transitive event with an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have lifted up' licenses an external argument (the implied agent 'you') and introduces an overt direct object ('the Son of man'), triggering set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'lifted up' licenses an internal argument ('the Son of man'), violating the requirement that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The act of lifting up/impaling involves a durative process phase (raising onto a stake) that culminates in a defined result state (being impaled/elevated), fitting the accomplishment profile.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have lifted up (impaled)' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (the Son of man being impaled/elevated), representing a definable threshold moment rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The situation entails a specific, inherent endpoint (the completion of lifting up/impaling the Son of man) and can be modified by 'in X time' (e.g., 'when you have lifted up in one hour'), indicating a bounded event with a defined result state rather than a dynamic activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have lifted up (impaled)' entails a resulting change of state—the Son of man transitions from not-impaled to impaled—which violates the semelfactive requirement of no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have lifted up' denotes a bounded dynamic event involving change-of-state (moving from ground to elevated position), which entails internal eventive unfolding incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents an open conditional where the lifting up of the Son of man is presented as a future possibility ('When you have lifted up...you will know'), not as contrary to fact, matching the set_to_0_if condition for open conditionals.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have lifted up (impaled)' is presented as a future event that will occur ('When you have lifted up...you will know'), not as absent or negated; no negator applies to this gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'When you have lifted up impaled the Son of man' contains a temporal clause ('When'), but this is a conditional/future-oriented temporal marker that does not establish a specific past time interval; it anchors to a hypothetical future event, not a definite past moment.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have lifted up (impaled)' entails a change of state (the Son of man being impaled/elevated), and this resulting state is relevant for the subsequent clause 'you will know that I am that person,' indicating the state persists at the reference time and is crucial for the discourse that follows.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'when you have lifted up impaled the Son of man' encodes a change of state (from not-impaled to impaled) with the resulting state persisting at the reference time of the consequent clause 'you will know that I am that person,' making it resultative.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the lifting up/impalement of the Son of man) that triggers a consequent state ('you will know'), focusing on the transition and completion of the action rather than a continuous situation holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have lifted up' presents the transition of impalement as a bounded, completed event construed as a whole, meeting the intuition criterion for TLAspPfv; none of the set_to_0_if conditions apply.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'When you have lifted up the Son of man' is embedded within a conditional temporal structure ('When...you will know') that projects a future consequence, construing the lifting up as a hypothetical event whose realization triggers a subsequent state, rather than asserting it as an already-realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have lifted up' is a present perfect form embedded in a future temporal context ('when you have lifted up...you will know'), which constitutes a tense-shifted perfect construction distinct from the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have lifted up' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'you' as the semantic agent performing the action of lifting; there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle sequence) and the object 'the Son of man' is the semantic patient in object position, not subject position.

FullText: » He sent me and is with me . He has not left me alone . I always do the things that are pleasing to him ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has not left' introduces an overt internal argument ('me'), satisfying the two-participant transitive event structure with an external argument (He) and an affected internal argument (me).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'left' licenses an external argument (the subject 'He' as agent) and introduces an overt direct object ('me'), violating both set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'left' licenses an internal argument ('me'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Has not left' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the act of leaving/departing) without a durative process phase leading to a defined culmination point, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'has not left me alone' describes a state of continuous presence rather than a punctual transition; it unfolds over measurable duration and does not represent a single temporal boundary crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The situation entails a specific result state (not having left) and marks the onset of a bounded state of non-abandonment, functioning as a completed perceptual/cognitive event rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has not left me alone' entails a resulting state (remaining in a state of not being alone) and unfolds over a measurable duration from past to present, making it durative rather than punctual/semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has not left me alone' is marked with progressive perfect aspect ('has...left'), denoting a change-of-state event (departure/abandonment) with an inherent endpoint, which involves dynamic change incompatible with AktState.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has not left' contains direct syntactic negation ('not') that negates the eventuality of leaving, presenting the state as not occurring.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'He has not left me alone' contains no overt temporal adverbial (lexical, phrasal, or clausal) that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'leave' entails a change of location/state, and the negated perfect 'has not left' asserts that the resulting state (remaining present/with the subject) holds at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The negated perfect 'has not left me alone' entails a resulting state (continuous presence/companionship) that persists at the reference time, indicating a transition from a state of separation to one of ongoing togetherness.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'has not left me alone' expresses a continuous state (non-abandonment) holding from a past point to the reference time, matching the intuition of a situation maintained across a time span rather than a discrete bounded event.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'has not left me alone' contains 'not yet' negation (realized as 'has not'), which indicates the transition has not occurred and the situation remains open, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has not left me alone' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world as a present perfect fact, and negation alone does not trigger irrealis per the edge_cases rule.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has not left' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'left' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has not left' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'He' as the semantic agent (doer) and 'me' as the grammatical object; it lacks the passive morphology (have + been + past participle) required for VcPass.

FullText: They answered him : » We are Abraham’s seed , and have never been in bondage to any man . How can you say we will be made free ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'be' functions as a copula linking the subject to a predicative complement ('in bondage'), not introducing an internal argument affected by an external agent, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for copular verbs.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('been in bondage') and the lexical verb 'be' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'any man' who would hold them in bondage), triggering the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses with external argument licensing.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'been' (copula) licenses an internal argument ('in bondage'), and the sole argument functions as a theme/patient rather than an external agent, violating the core requirement for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The expression 'have never been in bondage' denotes a stative situation (being in a state of bondage) that lacks an inherent endpoint or culminating result state, making it neither an accomplishment nor a dynamic process leading to a defined terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have never been in bondage' unfolds over a measurable duration (a state of servitude persisting over time) rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, and it describes a durative condition rather than a punctual transition into a new result state.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'have never been in bondage' denotes a defined result state (the condition of not being enslaved) rather than a dynamic durative activity requiring sustained engagement, and the perceptual/cognitive event of awareness of bondage status is treated as a single completed occurrence.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have never been in bondage' describes a stative condition of non-existence over an extended period (entire lifetime), not a single punctual event without resulting state change, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have never been in bondage' is marked with perfect aspect ('have been') which encodes a change-of-state or bounded condition (entering/being in a state of bondage), and 'in bondage' denotes a dynamic social/political condition involving constraint and loss of freedom, not a static durative property.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (the speakers claim they have never been in bondage as a factual statement about their history), without any indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have never been in bondage' contains the negator 'never' which directly negates the eventuality of being in bondage, presenting the state as not occurring or not holding.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains 'never', which is a vague/negation adverbial rather than an overt temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; no lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial is present.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'be in bondage' entails a change of state (entering a condition of servitude), and the negative perfect 'have never been in bondage' asserts that this resulting state has never occurred, which directly contradicts the set_to_0_if condition about state-change verbs; moreover, the clause emphasizes the absence of a persistent condition rather than a life-history occurrence.
DtRes_explanation: The negated clause 'have never been in bondage' does not entail a resulting state; negation blocks the transition to a result state, and the stative copula 'been' describes a condition rather than encoding a change of state.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have never been in bondage' describes a state of non-occurrence (never experiencing bondage) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the negation with 'never' emphasizes the absence of discrete events rather than continuous duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have never been in bondage' denotes a stative situation (the state of not being in bondage) without a construed boundary or transition, and the negation with 'never' makes the internal temporal span accessible rather than packaging it as a bounded whole event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have never been in bondage' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world as a factual claim by the speakers about their own history, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applying.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have never been in bondage' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle ('been'), with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the prepositional phrase 'in bondage', a locative/state predicate rather than a verbal past participle, making 'been' a copula (copular construction) not a passive auxiliary.

FullText: » I speak the things that I have seen and heard when I was with my Father . You also do the things that you heard from your father . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have seen' introduces an overt internal argument ('the things'), satisfying the intuition of a two-participant transitive event with an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have seen' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I' as agent/experiencer) and takes a direct object ('the things'), violating both the external argument and overt object conditions for ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have seen' licenses an internal argument ('the things'), violating the core requirement that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have seen' denotes a perceptual event construed as a punctual transition into awareness/perception without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, classifying it as an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (instantaneous discovery of visual information) rather than a durative process, matching the edge case for cognitive/perceptual verbs and the positive example 'They have heard the news.'
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing/perceiving something), which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative activity, matching the negative example 'They have heard the blasphemy.'
AktSemel_explanation: The perfect aspect 'have seen' construes the event as a state resulting from a prior occurrence (a change from not-having-seen to having-seen), entailing a resulting state rather than a punctual event without consequence.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (perceiving/witnessing something), which is explicitly listed in set_to_0_if as incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have seen and heard' presents situations that actually occurred in the past (factual experience), not a counterfactual condition, and lacks any morphological or contextual markers indicating the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have seen' is not negated; it expresses a positive eventuality that is realized, and no negator in the sentence applies to this gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'I have seen and heard' contains no overt temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'when I was with my Father' is a subordinate temporal clause modifying the broader context, not an adverbial directly anchoring the main clause.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have seen' presents the event as part of the subject's life history (things witnessed in the past with the Father) without asserting that a resulting state currently holds; it answers whether the event occurred, matching the positive example 'We have seen the Eiffel Tower.'
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have seen' reports a past perceptual event without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it is used to describe past experience that grounds present speech, not to indicate a change of state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have seen and heard when I was with my Father' describes discrete bounded events (specific things witnessed during a past period) rather than a continuous situation holding from past to present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have seen' presents the bounded completion of a perceptual transition (coming to see/perceive) as a whole, without profiling internal temporal development, and the clause does not contain duration adverbials or negation that would make the internal span accessible.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have seen' asserts a realized experience in the actual world (the speaker has actually witnessed things with the Father), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have seen' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'seen' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have seen' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent (doer) and the implied object as the semantic patient; there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle sequence).

FullText: » I speak the things that I have seen and heard when I was with my Father . You also do the things that you heard from your father . «

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'I' who performs the hearing), violating the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb must not license an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an internal argument (the things that were heard), violating the first set_to_0_if condition that requires no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Heard' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous perceptual transition into awareness without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, classifying it as an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (matching the positive example 'They have heard the news'), representing a single boundary moment of coming to know something rather than an unfolding durative process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness, which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative activity, matching the negative example 'They have heard the blasphemy.'
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have heard' entails a resulting state of knowledge/perception and unfolds over a duration (the time spent with the Father), not a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of awareness (hearing something), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if rule for perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have seen and heard when I was with my Father' presents situations that actually occurred in the past (factual events), not contrary-to-fact situations, so it does not instantiate ClCntfcl.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have heard' is not negated; the sentence presents the hearing as a realized event that occurred in the past, with no negator applying to this eventuality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'I have seen and heard' contains no overt temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'when I was with my Father' modifies the broader context but does not directly anchor this particular clause.
DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'have heard' presents the event as part of the subject's life history (things experienced when with the Father) without asserting that a resulting state currently holds; it answers whether the event occurred, not whether a state persists.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have heard' reports a past perceptual event without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it is an experiential perfect describing what was perceived in the past, not a transition to a new state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'have seen and heard when I was with my Father' describes discrete perceptual events that occurred during a bounded past period ('when I was with my Father'), not a continuous situation holding from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'heard' denotes a stative situation (perception/knowledge state) without a construed boundary, and the clause does not introduce a transition into or out of that state, matching the set_to_0_if condition for stative situations.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have heard' asserts a realized past experience ('things that I have seen and heard when I was with my Father') as an actual fact, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applying.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'heard' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have heard' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent (doer) and lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: Jesus said : » If God were your Father you would love me . I came from God ! I have not come from myself . He sent me !
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' in 'have not come' is intransitive and introduces no overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it only has an overt external argument (the implicit subject 'I') and a prepositional adjunct ('from myself'), which does not satisfy the requirement for an internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' encodes a one-participant change-of-location event where the sole argument (implicit 'I') functions as an internal argument (theme), with no external argument licensed or overtly realized.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (implicit subject 'I') functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing motion, not as an external argument/agent, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have not come' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival/origination) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have not come' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (arrival/presence at a location), representing a definable threshold crossing regardless of any preparatory journey, consistent with the positive example 'They have reached the summit.'
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'have not come from myself' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (the act of coming/originating) and denotes a bounded perceptual or cognitive event marking the onset of awareness or a completed occurrence, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have not come' entails a transition into a resulting state (arrival/presence) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence but rather as a negated state of completion, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have not come' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset or completion of an action (coming/arrival), which involves dynamic change and eventive progression, thus failing the stative condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have not come from myself' presents a factual statement about what actually occurred (Jesus's actual origin), not a situation contrary to fact, and thus does not instantiate the counterfactual feature.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have not come' is directly negated by 'not', which applies to the eventuality of coming, presenting the event as not occurring.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'I have not come from myself' contains no overt temporal adverbial (lexical, phrasal, or clausal) that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The negative perfect 'have not come from myself' presents the non-occurrence of an event as part of the subject's life history without asserting a resulting state, matching the pattern in examples_positive ('He had not sinned').
DtRes_explanation: The clause negates the occurrence of the event ('have not come'), and negation of an event entails no transition to a resulting state, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have not come from myself' describes a negated transition/origin event (coming from a source) rather than a continuous state or activity holding over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion about transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have not come from myself' contains 'not yet' negation (realized as 'have not'), which indicates the transition has not occurred and the situation remains open, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have not come' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (Jesus's origin), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation; negation alone does not trigger irrealis per edge_cases.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have not come' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have not come' is active voice with the semantic agent (Jesus) as grammatical subject and no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); it lacks the required auxiliary sequence for passive voice.

FullText: The Jews replied : » You are not yet fifty years old . Have you seen Abraham ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have seen' introduces an overt internal argument 'Abraham' (direct object), satisfying the intuition of a two-participant transitive event directed from the external argument toward an affected internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you') and introduces an overt direct object 'Abraham', triggering set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an internal argument (Abraham as direct object), violating the requirement for zero internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have you seen Abraham?' construes seeing as a punctual perceptual transition into visual awareness rather than a durative process leading to a defined endpoint, classifying it as an achievement (0).
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (seeing Abraham), which is a single instantaneous boundary event regardless of any preparatory looking process, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news.'
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing Abraham), which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktSemel_explanation: The perfective 'have seen' entails a resulting state of knowledge/experience and does not construe the event as a single punctual occurrence, failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing Abraham), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if rule for perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause is an embedded interrogative introduced by 'Have you' (direct question about actual experience), not a counterfactual condition, so it presents uncertain truth rather than a situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have seen' is a question without negation; the negator 'not' in the sentence applies to 'are' ("You are not yet fifty years old"), not to the target gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'Have you seen Abraham?' contains no overt temporal adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval; 'yet' is a logical/aspectual modifier, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'Have you seen Abraham?' queries whether the addressee has encountered Abraham at any point in their life history, focusing on occurrence rather than a resulting state, matching the positive example 'Have you ever tried sushi?'
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have seen' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it merely reports a past experiential event without a change of state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (seeing Abraham) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the question format emphasizes the occurrence of a specific event rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'Have you seen Abraham?' is negated with 'not yet' in the preceding context ('You are not yet fifty years old'), indicating the transition has not occurred and the situation remains open, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The interrogative form 'Have you seen Abraham?' projects a hypothetical or impossible outcome (seeing a biblical figure from centuries past), which construes the situation as non-actual rather than asserting a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have seen' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'seen' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have seen' is active voice (subject 'you' is the semantic agent performing the action of seeing), not passive voice; passive voice requires the morphological sequence 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: The Jews did not believe that he had been blind and then received his sight . So they called his parents .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'be' functions as a copula linking the subject 'he' to the predicative complement 'blind', rather than introducing an internal argument, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for copular verbs.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'had been blind' is a copular construction where 'blind' encodes a one-participant state with the subject as the sole internal argument (theme), licensing no external argument and containing no overt object.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'blind' denotes a change-of-state event where the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) rather than an external argument, matching the set_to_0_if condition for unaccusative verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Had been blind' describes a stative condition (blindness) rather than a dynamic event with a durative process phase leading to a defined terminal point, so it does not instantiate AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The state of being blind is a durative condition that unfolds over time rather than a punctual transition into a new result state, and the pluperfect 'had been blind' emphasizes the extended duration of this state prior to the recovery event.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'had been blind' denotes a state without dynamic unfolding or sustained engagement, lacking the durative activity character required for AktAct; it is a stative condition, not an activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been blind' entails a resulting state (blindness as a condition) and unfolds over measurable duration rather than as a single punctual occurrence, violating the semelfactive requirement.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been blind' is morphologically marked as progressive aspect (past perfect progressive), which according to set_to_0_if entails dynamic unfolding and is incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had been blind' presents a factual situation that actually occurred in the narrative world (the man was indeed blind and then received sight), using past perfect for temporal anteriority within the narrative, not to express a counterfactual situation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negator 'did not' negates the matrix verb 'believe', not the target gram 'had been blind', which is embedded in the complement clause and remains positive in its assertion.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'had been blind' contains no overt temporal adverbial anchoring it to a specific past time interval; 'then' appears in the following clause and refers to the subsequent event, not to the timing of blindness.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had been blind' entails a state (blindness) that held at the reference time and is relevant to the narrative context (contrasted with 'received his sight'), making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'had been blind' is a stative copula construction describing a prior state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state that persists at reference time; the edge_cases rule explicitly states that copular constructions do not encode transitions.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'he had been blind' expresses a state that held continuously from a past starting point until the transition marked by 'and then received his sight', fitting the intuition of a situation holding across a time span before a change occurs.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had been blind' denotes a stative situation (the state of blindness) without a construed boundary; the clause does not introduce a transition into or out of that state, matching the set_to_0_if condition for statives.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'he had been blind' is embedded within a reported belief context ('did not believe that'), but it asserts a realized fact about the actual world—the man's prior condition of blindness—rather than projecting a hypothetical, conditional, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been blind' is a past perfect construction (had + past participle 'been'), which encodes past tense rather than the simple present perfect, thus instantiating TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the adjective 'blind' (a non-verbal predicate), making this a copular construction rather than a passive voice construction; passive voice requires 'been' + past participle functioning as a verbal passive.

FullText: The Jews did not believe that he had been blind and then received his sight . So they called his parents .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'received' introduces an overt internal argument ('his sight'), encoding a two-participant event where the subject receives/is affected by the object, satisfying the ASTrns feature.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'received' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'he' who actively received/obtained his sight), violating the core requirement that ASUncc verbs must not license external arguments.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'received' licenses an internal argument ('his sight' as direct object), violating the core requirement that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Received his sight' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a new perceptual state (gaining sight) without a durative process phase, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'received his sight' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (from blindness to sightedness), representing the instantaneous boundary point of change rather than an unfolding process, consistent with the achievement feature AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'received his sight' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (the moment sight was regained) and a defined result state (having sight), matching the set_to_0_if condition for bounded events with inherent endpoints.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'received his sight' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from blindness to sightedness), which violates the set_to_0_if condition and disqualifies it as semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had received his sight' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (regaining vision) involving dynamic progression from blindness to sightedness, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (he did receive his sight), without any indication that it is contrary to fact; the Jews' disbelief does not make the event counterfactual.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negator 'did not believe' negates the belief relation, not the target gram 'had received his sight', which is presented as a realized event within the embedded clause.
DtDTAM_explanation: The adverbial 'then' in this context means 'therefore' or 'as a result' (logical connector), not 'at that time' (temporal anchor), so the clause does not contain an overt temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'received his sight' entails a change of state (from blind to sighted) where the resulting state holds at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'had received his sight' encodes a transition from blindness to sightedness, with the resulting state (being able to see) persisting at the reference time of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (receiving sight as a transition into a new state) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition on state transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'had received his sight' presents a bounded transition (regaining vision) as a completed whole event, construing the transition itself as a single accomplished fact rather than its internal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'he had been blind and then received his sight' is embedded in a reported belief context ('The Jews did not believe that...'), but the situation itself is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world—the man actually was blind and actually received his sight; the negation applies only to the Jews' belief, not to the reality of the events.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had received' is a past perfect construction (past-tense 'had' + past participle 'received'), which encodes tense specification other than simple present perfect, triggering TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had received his sight' is active voice (agent subject 'he' performs the action of receiving); it lacks the passive morphology 'been' + past participle required for VcPass.

FullText: The parents said these things because they feared the Jews . The Jews had agreed that if any man should confess him to be Christ , he should be put out of the synagogue .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' introduces an overt internal argument (the finite clause 'that if any man should confess him to be Christ, he should be put out of the synagogue'), which functions as the content of the agreement and represents the affected theme of the two-participant event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' licenses an external argument (the Jews as agent/initiator) and introduces an overt indirect object (that-clause), violating both set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'agreed' licenses an internal argument (the finite clause 'that if any man should confess him to be Christ, he should be put out of the synagogue'), violating the requirement that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Had agreed' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a state of agreement without a durative process phase inherent to the verb itself, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had agreed' represents a punctual transition into a new result state (the Jews moving from disagreement to agreement), construed as a single boundary point rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' denotes a bounded cognitive event marking the onset of a mutual decision with a defined result state (the agreement itself), lacking dynamic durative unfolding and fitting the pattern of bounded perceptual/cognitive events that mark awareness or commitment.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had agreed' entails a transition into a resulting state (a commitment or decision that persists), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (the moment of reaching agreement), which involves dynamic progression toward a completion point, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state verbs.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'The Jews had agreed that if any man should confess him to be Christ, he should be put out of the synagogue' uses past perfect to express a factual prior event (the Jews' actual agreement) within narrative sequence, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had agreed' expresses a positive eventuality (an agreement that occurred); no negator negates this gram—the sentence structure presents the agreement as realized, not absent.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'The Jews had agreed that if any man should confess him to be Christ, he should be put out of the synagogue' contains no overt temporal adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval; the 'if' clause is conditional, not temporal.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' entails a change in social/legal status (the Jews collectively established a binding agreement/decision) that remains in effect at the reference time, as evidenced by the subsequent conditional clause describing the ongoing consequence of that agreement.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' encodes a transition to a resulting state (a mutual decision/commitment) that persists at the reference time, entailing that the agreement remains in effect when the parents made their statement.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' describes a discrete bounded event (a decision/agreement made at a specific past point) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for transitions into new states.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'had agreed' presents a completed agreement as a bounded whole event (a transition into the state of having made an agreement), with no internal temporal development profiled, matching the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had agreed' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (the Jews' prior agreement), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had agreed' is a past perfect construction (had + past participle), which encodes past tense marking rather than the simple present perfect form, thus instantiating TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'The Jews' is the semantic agent (doer) of the agreement, and 'had agreed' is active voice with no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle structure).

FullText: » No one takes it away from me , but I lay it down voluntarily . I have authority jurisdiction right to lay it down , and I have authority to take it again . I have received this commandment from my Father . «
